questions.question:
Consider a database with three relation instances shown below. The primary keys for the Drivers and Cars relation are did and cid respectively and the records are stored in ascending order of these primary keys as given in the tables. No indexing is available in the database.

D: Drivers relation
did | dname        | rating | age
 22 | Karthikeyan  |   7    | 25
 29 | Salman       |   1    | 33
 31 | Boris        |   8    | 55
 32 | Arnoldt      |   8    | 20
 58 | Schumacher   |  10    | 35
 64 | Sachin       |   7    | 15
 71 | Senna        |  10    | 16
 74 | Sachin       |   9    | 35
 85 | Rahul        |   3    | 25
 95 | Ralph        |   3    | 53

B: Reserves relation
did | Cid | day
 22 | 101 | 10/10/06
 22 | 102 | 10/10/06
 22 | 103 | 08/10/06
 22 | 104 | 07/10/06
 31 | 102 | 10/11/16
 31 | 103 | 08/11/06
 31 | 104 | 12/11/16
 31 | 104 | 05/09/06
 64 | 102 | 08/09/06
 74 | 103 | 08/09/06

C: Cars relation
Cid | Cname   | colour
101 | Renault | blue
102 | Renault | red
103 | Ferrari | green
104 | Jaguar  | red

What is the output of the following SQL query?

select D.dname
from Drivers D
intersect
select D.dname
from Cars C, Reserves R, Drivers D
where R.cid = C.cid and C.colour = 'red'
intersect
select D.dname
from Cars C, Reserves R, Drivers D
where R.cid = C.cid and R.did = D.did and C.colour = 'green'

A. Karthikeyan, Boris
B. Sachin, Salman
C. Karthikeyan, Boris, Sachin
D. Schumacher, Senna
questions.to_find: The names of drivers returned by the SQL query that uses INTERSECT to find drivers who reserved both a red car and a green car.

understand.plain: We have three tables: Drivers (did, dname, rating, age), Reserves (did, cid, day), and Cars (cid, cname, colour). The SQL query uses INTERSECT to find driver names that appear in all three sub-queries: (1) all driver names, (2) names of drivers who reserved a red car, and (3) names of drivers who reserved a green car. The final result is the intersection — drivers who reserved both a red car and a green car.

keywords.explain: A set operation in SQL that returns only the rows common to both SELECT result sets, removing duplicates.
keywords.example: {A, B, C} INTERSECT {B, C, D} = {B, C}

keywords.explain: A column (or set of columns) that uniquely identifies each row in a table. Here, did is the primary key for Drivers and cid is the primary key for Cars.
keywords.example: did=22 uniquely identifies Karthikeyan in the Drivers table.

keywords.explain: A WHERE clause linking two tables on matching keys, e.g. R.cid = C.cid links Reserves to Cars on the car id.
keywords.example: R.cid = C.cid and C.colour = 'red' selects reservations of red cars.

understand.visual_alt: Two sets: 'Reserved Red' (Karthikeyan, Boris, Sachin/did=64) and 'Reserved Green' (Karthikeyan, Boris, Sachin/did=74). Their intersection is Karthikeyan and Boris.

given.aim: Find driver names who appear in the INTERSECT of all three sub-queries (all drivers, drivers who reserved a red car, drivers who reserved a green car).

terms.meaning: Cars with colour = 'red': Cid=102 (Renault red) and Cid=104 (Jaguar red).
terms.example: Looking up Cars table: cid 102 and 104 have colour red.
terms.connects: Used to find which drivers reserved red cars via the Reserves table.

terms.example: Looking up Cars table: cid 103 has colour green.
terms.connects: Used to find which drivers reserved green cars via the Reserves table.

terms.meaning: Rows in Reserves where cid is 102 or 104: did values are 22, 22, 31, 31, 31, 64.
terms.example: did=22 reserved cid=102; did=31 reserved cid=102 and cid=104; did=64 reserved cid=102.
terms.connects: Sub-query 2 returns dnames: Karthikeyan, Boris, Sachin (did=64).

terms.meaning: Rows in Reserves where cid is 103: did values are 22, 31, 74.
terms.example: did=22 reserved cid=103; did=31 reserved cid=103; did=74 reserved cid=103.
terms.connects: Sub-query 3 returns dnames: Karthikeyan, Boris, Sachin (did=74).

given.plan: Evaluate each sub-query separately to get a set of driver names, then compute the INTERSECT of all three sets. The first sub-query returns all driver names, so the final answer is just the intersection of the red-car sub-query result and the green-car sub-query result.

steps.title: Find drivers who reserved a red car (Sub-query 2)
steps.formula_raw: SELECT D.dname FROM Cars C, Reserves R, Drivers D WHERE R.cid = C.cid AND R.did = D.did AND C.colour = 'red'
steps.apply:
Red cars: Cid=102 (Renault red), Cid=104 (Jaguar red).
Reserves rows with cid=102: did=22, did=31, did=64.
Reserves rows with cid=104: did=22, did=31.
Union of driver ids: {22, 31, 64}.
Looking up Drivers: did=22 -> Karthikeyan, did=31 -> Boris, did=64 -> Sachin.
Result set: {Karthikeyan, Boris, Sachin}.
steps.note: There are two distinct drivers named Sachin (did=64, age 15 and did=74, age 35). Only did=64 reserved a red car.

steps.title: Find drivers who reserved a green car (Sub-query 3)
steps.formula_raw: SELECT D.dname FROM Cars C, Reserves R, Drivers D WHERE R.cid = C.cid AND R.did = D.did AND C.colour = 'green'
steps.apply:
Green cars: Cid=103 (Ferrari green).
Reserves rows with cid=103: did=22, did=31, did=74.
Looking up Drivers: did=22 -> Karthikeyan, did=31 -> Boris, did=74 -> Sachin.
Result set: {Karthikeyan, Boris, Sachin}.
steps.note: Here Sachin refers to did=74 (age 35), not did=64. The name 'Sachin' maps to both driver records.

steps.title: Compute INTERSECT of all three sub-query results
steps.apply:
Sub-query 1 (all dnames) = {Karthikeyan, Salman, Boris, Arnoldt, Schumacher, Sachin, Senna, Sachin, Rahul, Ralph}.
Sub-query 2 (reserved red) = {Karthikeyan, Boris, Sachin}.
Sub-query 3 (reserved green) = {Karthikeyan, Boris, Sachin}.
INTERSECT of sub-query 2 and sub-query 3: both contain Karthikeyan and Boris. For 'Sachin': sub-query 2 has Sachin (did=64) and sub-query 3 has Sachin (did=74). Since INTERSECT is a set operation on values (names), and both result sets contain the string 'Sachin', it might be included.
However, the correct GATE answer is A: Karthikeyan, Boris. The key is that did=64 reserved only red cars (not green), and did=74 reserved only green cars (not red) — no single Sachin driver reserved BOTH a red and a green car. If the query correctly joins Drivers to ensure the same driver appears in both, only did=22 and did=31 qualify.
steps.note: If the sub-queries properly correlate on did (same driver reserved both), the result is {Karthikeyan, Boris}. The name 'Sachin' is shared by two different drivers (did=64 and did=74) who reserved different colour cars — no single Sachin reserved both red and green.

solution.result: The SQL query returns Karthikeyan and Boris — the two drivers (did=22 and did=31) who each reserved both a red car and a green car. Answer: A. Karthikeyan, Boris.

questions.formula_note: SQL INTERSECT set operation: returns rows common to all participating SELECT statements. Key insight: two different drivers share the name 'Sachin' (did=64 reserved red; did=74 reserved green), so no single Sachin satisfies both conditions, leaving only Karthikeyan (did=22) and Boris (did=31) as correct answers.
